titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Trinity Test Collection on the Manhattan Project Medical Group
Trinity Test Collection on the Manhattan Project Medical Group
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Joleen Degradi

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday October 12, 2023 at 03:02

did.unittitle: Trinity Test Collection on the Manhattan Project Medical Group

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Barry Lawrence Ruderman Antique Maps, Inc. on the Frederick W. and Carrie S. Beinecke Fund for Western Americana, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Material is organized into four series as follows: Series I: Preparing for Trinity (1945 April, 12-1945 July, 15), Series II: Day of Trinity (1945 July,16-17), Series III: The Fallout (1945 July, 17-1946 March, 9), and Series IV: Maps and Diagrams (1945 May – 1945 August, 8).

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The Manhattan Project Medical Group was a group of physicians hired by United States Army General Leslie Groves a few months before The Trinity test (July 16th, 1945). General Leslie Groves was the director of the Manhattan Project. Other members of the Medical Group included physicians Stafford L. Warren, James F. Nolan, Joe Hoffman, and Lewis Hempelmann. The Trinity test was a purposeful detonation of an atomic bomb prototype, code named The Gadget, to observe the explosion, resulting detonation cloud, and post-detonation radiation. The results from the Trinity test lead to last minute adjustments and preparations for one of the bombs dropped on the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August, 1945.

The Medical Group was established due to concerns raised by scientists and military lawyers of the Manhattan Project about radiation exposure to personnel on site, nearby residents, and the environment. As head of the Medical Group, Stafford L. Warren had the closest relationship with General Leslie Groves and was the one to gain approval for a monitoring plan on Trinity. The Medical Group was given a limited budget to prepare for possible radiation poisoning, detonanation cloud tracking, and preparation for possible evacuation of military personnel and civilians within range of the detonanation cloud should the wind push it in their direction.

While the Manhattan Project was formed in 1942 by President Franklin D. Roosevelt, it was not until The Smyth Report was released August 12, 1945, that the American public was made aware of the Manhattan Project's existence and development of nuclear weapons and their use in bombing Hiroshima and Nagasaki during World War II.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Items have been assigned a unit ID number that reflects the numbers given by the dealer in the pamphlet "Zero Hour: Documenting the Dawn of the Nuclear Age, The Trinity Test Archive of the Manhattan Project Medical Group". There is a copy of this pamphlet in the first folder of each box for researcher use.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection consists of memoranda, scientific sketches, graphs, notes, and reports related to Trinity, the code name of the first detonation of a nuclear weapon, conducted by the United States Army on July 16, 1945 at the White Sands Proving Ground in New Mexico, as part of the Manhattan Project. Memoranda in the collection were created by the Manhattan Project Medical Group in preparation of the Trinity test, and provide instructions to military personnel stationed near and around the test site for tracking the detonation cloud following the blast. Reports from meetings in preparation for the test illustrate concerns that the Medical Group had about the test. Sketches and graphs in the collection were created in real time during the detonation, and detail preparations for the possibility of evacuating military and civilan personnel nearby the test site.

Maps and diagrams in the collection were used for planning and stationing purposes leading up to Trinity, tracking of the detonation cloud alongside placement of personnel observing the test, the movement of the cloud,and radiation levels in the surrounding detonation area during the days after Trinity. Many of these maps have markings of potentially dangerous areas due to radiation levels and cloud movement after the detonation.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Trinity Test Collection on the Manhattan Project Medical Group is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.
